laws.summary: Every arrested person must be told the grounds of arrest as soon as possible, has the right to consult and be defended by a lawyer of their choice, and must be produced before the nearest magistrate within 24 hours of arrest (excluding travel time).
Police must inform the arrested person of the grounds of arrest and their right to bail (for bailable offences), and must also inform a friend/relative of the arrest and place of detention.
Requires police to maintain and display, at every police station, a list of names and addresses of persons currently under arrest, and the grounds for arrest.
Sets limits on arrest without warrant; for offences punishable with less than 3 years imprisonment, arrest generally requires a notice of appearance first, subject to conditions.

laws.section: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal (1997)
laws.summary: Laid down mandatory safeguards for arrest and detention: memo of arrest attested by a witness, right to inform a relative/friend, medical examination every 48 hours, and the arrestee's right to meet their lawyer during interrogation.
Wrongful confinement/restraint by anyone, including a public servant acting outside legal authority, is a punishable offence.

steps: Note the exact time, place, and names/badge numbers of the officers involved, if possible.
Ask explicitly for the written grounds of arrest and the arrest memo (must be signed by a witness).
Insist that a relative or friend be informed of your arrest and location, as required under BNSS Section 47.
You must be produced before a magistrate within 24 hours (excluding travel time) — track this.
If these steps were not followed, you can file a written complaint with the Superintendent of Police (SP) or approach the State/District Legal Services Authority for free legal aid.
You may also file a habeas corpus petition in the High Court if detention continues without being produced before a magistrate.
Consider filing a complaint with the State Human Rights Commission for procedural violations.
Consult a criminal lawyer promptly; NALSA (nalsa.gov.in) provides free legal aid for those who qualify.

laws.summary: Police are legally required to register an FIR for any information disclosing a cognizable offence; for certain offences, a preliminary inquiry within 14 days may precede registration in limited circumstances.

laws.section: Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of U.P. (2014)
laws.summary: Held that registration of an FIR is mandatory when information discloses a cognizable offence; refusal to register is not permitted merely because facts are disputed.
If police refuse to register an FIR, the complainant can send the complaint in writing to the Superintendent of Police, who must investigate or direct an officer to do so.

steps: Request the FIR in writing and ask for the copy along with the FIR number.
If refused, send a written complaint by post/email to the Superintendent of Police (SP) under BNSS Section 175.
You can also approach the Judicial Magistrate directly under BNSS Section 175(4) to direct police to register the FIR.
For crimes committed in one place but reported elsewhere, ask for a 'Zero FIR', which must be registered and later transferred to the appropriate police station.
File a complaint with the State Human Rights Commission or approach the High Court via writ petition if there is continued refusal.
Keep all written correspondence, screenshots, or acknowledgment receipts as evidence of your attempt to file.

laws.summary: Corresponds to the earlier IPC Section 498A — cruelty by husband or his relatives towards a woman is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine; cruelty includes harassment for dowry.
Giving, taking, or demanding dowry is a punishable offence, with imprisonment and fine for both giving and demanding dowry.

laws.act: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
laws.summary: Allows a woman to seek protection orders, residence orders, and monetary relief from a Magistrate against domestic violence, including dowry-related harassment.

Corresponds to former IPC 304B — dowry death; if a woman dies within 7 years of marriage under suspicious circumstances linked to dowry harassment, it is presumed to be dowry death unless proven otherwise.

steps: Document incidents with dates, messages, witnesses, and medical records if injured.
File a complaint at the nearest police station or the Women's Helpline (181) / National Commission for Women.
You can approach a Protection Officer under the Domestic Violence Act, 2005 for immediate protection and residence orders.
File an application before a Magistrate for protection, monetary relief, or residence orders under the DV Act.
Approach the Family Court or file for maintenance if separated.
Contact NALSA or your District Legal Services Authority for free legal representation.
Note: false 498A/dowry complaints are also punishable, so ensure your complaint reflects facts accurately.

laws.summary: Dishonour of a cheque due to insufficient funds is a criminal offence, punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fine up to twice the cheque amount.

The payee must send a written demand notice to the drawer within 30 days of receiving the bank's dishonour memo, demanding payment within 15 days of notice receipt, before filing a criminal complaint.
A complaint must be filed within 1 month of the cause of action arising (i.e., after the 15-day notice period lapses without payment), before a Magistrate.

steps: Obtain the cheque return memo from your bank stating the reason for dishonour.
Send a written legal demand notice to the drawer within 30 days of the dishonour memo, demanding payment within 15 days.
If payment is not made within 15 days of notice receipt, file a criminal complaint under Section 138 within 1 month before the Judicial Magistrate having jurisdiction.
You may also pursue a civil recovery suit for the amount owed, separately or in parallel.
Consult a lawyer to draft the demand notice and complaint correctly, as procedural timelines are strictly enforced.

topic: Consumer Rights / Defective Product or Service

laws.summary: Defines consumer rights including the right to safety, right to information, right to be heard, and right to seek redressal against unfair trade practices.
Allows a consumer to file a complaint before the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission for claims up to ₹50 lakh (limits are periodically revised).
Established the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) to investigate and act against unfair trade practices and misleading advertisements.

steps: First send a written complaint to the seller/service provider and preserve all communication, bills, and warranty documents.
File a complaint on the National Consumer Helpline (1915 or consumerhelpline.gov.in) or the INGRAM portal.
If unresolved, file a formal complaint before the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (for claims up to ₹50 lakh) — this can now be done online via e-daakhil.nic.in.
For e-commerce or unfair trade practice issues, you can also complain to the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA).
Consumer cases generally do not require a lawyer and are designed to be filed by individuals directly.

laws.act: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
laws.summary: Defines domestic violence broadly to include physical, sexual, verbal, emotional, and economic abuse by a person in a domestic relationship.

laws.act: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
laws.summary: Gives a woman the right to reside in the shared household regardless of ownership title, even if she has no legal right/title in the property.

laws.act: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
laws.summary: A Magistrate can pass protection orders (stopping further abuse), residence orders, and monetary relief orders (for medical expenses, loss of earnings, maintenance).
Cruelty by a husband or his relatives is a separate criminal offence carrying imprisonment up to 3 years, alongside the civil remedies under the DV Act.

steps: Reach out to a Protection Officer (appointed under the DV Act in every district) or a registered service provider/NGO for immediate support.
You can approach the Magistrate directly (via a Domestic Incident Report) for a protection order, residence order, and monetary relief — a lawyer is not mandatory to file.
Call the Women's Helpline (181) for 24x7 support and emergency assistance.
File a police complaint separately if there is physical assault, under BNS provisions on hurt/cruelty.
Document incidents: dates, injuries (get medical examination), messages, witnesses.
Contact NALSA or your District Legal Services Authority for free legal aid if you cannot afford a lawyer.

laws.summary: Unauthorized access to a computer system, downloading data, or introducing viruses makes the offender liable to pay compensation to the affected person.
Computer-related offences (hacking, unauthorized access causing wrongful loss) are punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine up to ₹5 lakh.

Identity theft — fraudulent use of another person's electronic signature, password, or unique identification (e.g. stolen UPI/bank credentials) is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹1 lakh.
Capturing, publishing, or transmitting images of a person's private area without consent violates privacy and is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years.
Publishing or transmitting obscene material in electronic form is punishable, with enhanced punishment for repeat offences.

Cheating (including online/digital cheating such as fake UPI requests or fraudulent transactions) is punishable, with enhanced punishment if it involves impersonation or is committed via electronic means.

steps: Report immediately on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal: cybercrime.gov.in, or call the Cyber Crime Helpline 1930 (especially urgent for financial fraud — faster reporting increases chances of freezing the transaction).
For financial fraud (UPI/bank), also inform your bank immediately to block further transactions and dispute the charge.
Preserve all evidence: screenshots, transaction IDs, messages, caller/sender details — do not delete anything.
File a police complaint/FIR at your local station or the nearest cyber cell if the online portal doesn't resolve it.
For harassment, morphed images, or sextortion, cybercrime.gov.in has a dedicated women/child-specific reporting section with faster response.
Consult a cyber law-focused advocate for serious financial loss or reputational harm cases.

topic: Traffic Violations / Road Accidents (Motor Vehicles Act)

laws.summary: Allows a victim (or their legal heirs, in case of death) of a motor vehicle accident to file a claim for compensation before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT).
Places a duty on the driver involved in an accident to stop, provide help/medical assistance if requested, and report the accident to police within 24 hours.
Provides for compensation on a 'no fault' basis (interim relief) for hit-and-run accidents through the Solatium Fund, even before fault is established.

Causing death by rash or negligent driving (culpable homicide not amounting to murder) carries criminal liability, with enhanced punishment for hit-and-run cases where the driver flees without reporting.

steps: At the accident scene: call for medical help first (108 ambulance), then inform the police (100/112) to register an FIR/accident report — this is essential for any compensation claim.
Take photos of the scene, vehicle damage, and note witness contact details if possible.
Get a Medico-Legal Certificate (MLC) if injured, and preserve all medical bills.

For compensation, file a claim before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) under Section 166 — you do not need to prove the other driver was necessarily convicted criminally to claim compensation.
For hit-and-run cases where the driver is unidentified, apply for compensation from the Solatium Fund via the police/District Collector's office.
Inform your own insurer promptly to preserve any own-damage or personal accident cover claims.
Consult a motor accident claims lawyer — many take such cases on a contingency basis.

topic: Right to Information (RTI) — Government Not Responding / Denying Information

laws.summary: Any citizen can request information from a public authority by submitting a written application (with prescribed fee) to the Public Information Officer (PIO).
The PIO must respond within 30 days of the request (48 hours if the matter concerns life or liberty); failure to respond in time is treated as a deemed refusal.

If information is denied or not provided in time, the applicant can file a first appeal with the departmental Appellate Authority within 30 days, and a second appeal with the State/Central Information Commission if still unresolved.
The Information Commission can penalize a PIO up to ₹250/day (max ₹25,000) for unreasonable delay, refusal, or providing incorrect/incomplete information without justification.

steps: File your RTI application in writing to the Public Information Officer of the relevant department, along with the prescribed fee (usually ₹10, waived for BPL applicants).
If no response within 30 days, or if you're unhappy with the response, file a First Appeal with the Appellate Authority of that department within 30 days.
If still unresolved, file a Second Appeal with the State or Central Information Commission (as applicable) — this can also address penalty for delay under Section 20.
RTI applications can now be filed online for central government departments via rtionline.gov.in; check for a state-specific portal for state departments.
Keep proof of submission (receipt/acknowledgment, tracking number, or postal proof) at every stage.
